title: Corporate Email Masking with High-Risk Recipient Redirect Pattern
narrative: Fraudster uses legitimate corporate P_emaildomain (microsoft.com, ibm.com, oracle.com) to pass initial email reputation checks, then redirects goods to a recently-created R_emaildomain on high-risk providers (tempmail.com, guerrillamail.com, mailinator.com). Typically targets digital goods or gift cards ($75-$500 range) with immediate fulfillment. The purchaser email shows 18+ months account history and clean transaction records, while recipient email was created within 72 hours. Pattern emerges when dist1 (distance between billing/shipping) exceeds 1500km despite domestic transaction. Card appears legitimate with normal card1_txn_count_7d (2-4 transactions), but R_emaildomain has zero prior transaction history in merchant database. Transactions cluster during business hours (txn_hour 9-17) to mimic corporate purchasing, but recipient domain MX records point to disposable email infrastructure. AVS shows full match, but CVV frequently missing on initial attempt.
indicators: P_emaildomain is established corporate domain (age >18 months), R_emaildomain is disposable service created within 72 hours
dist1 exceeds 1500km despite both addresses in same country code
TransactionAmt between $75-$500 targeting ProductCD = 'C' (digital goods) or gift cards
R_emaildomain MX records resolve to known temporary email providers (SPF/DKIM validation fails)
P_emaildomain shows 15+ successful prior transactions, R_emaildomain has zero merchant history
CVV match failure on first attempt, followed by successful retry within 8 minutes
Transactions occur during business hours (txn_hour 9-17) on weekdays only
summary: The purchaser's email is from a well-established corporate domain with a long history of clean transactions, but the recipient email was created within the last 72 hours on a disposable email service, and the shipping distance exceeds 1500km despite being a domestic order.
reasoning: The fraudster is exploiting the trust associated with legitimate corporate email addresses to bypass initial fraud checks, then redirecting digital goods or gift cards to a throwaway email address they control, making it nearly impossible to trace or recover the stolen merchandise once delivered.
indicator_explanations: Legitimate corporate sender email paired with brand-new throwaway recipient address
Sender and recipient locations impossibly far apart despite sharing country
Amount targets sweet spot for digital goods or gift card fraud
Recipient email fails authentication and routes through temporary provider infrastructure
Sender has clean transaction history while recipient has never bought anything
First CVV attempt fails then succeeds minutes later suggesting guessing attack
Activity confined to standard business hours on weekdays mimicking legitimate patterns
